title: Rhabdomyolysis
oneLiner: Muscle cell death releasing intracellular contents, causing AKI, life-threatening hyperkalaemia, and compartment syndrome; often from crush, statins, or extreme exertion.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Hyperkalaemia** – ECG: tall peaked T waves, wide QRS, sine wave → cardiac arrest. Single finding: ECG changes despite normal K+ (rapid release, check urgently). Treat immediately without waiting for labs.
- **Acute compartment syndrome** – Tense, swollen muscle compartment; pain on passive stretch; paraesthesia or reduced pulse (late). Single finding: pain out of proportion to clinical appearance. Needs immediate surgical fasciotomy referral.
sections.Presentation:
- Cardinal symptoms: severe muscle pain (often thighs, calves, lower back), weakness, and dark red-brown/tea-coloured urine
- Key exam findings: muscle tenderness and swelling; signs of hypovolaemia (tachycardia, dry mucous membranes, oliguria); check for compartment syndrome (tense swelling, pain on stretch, neurovascular compromise)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Rhabdomyolysis** (CK very high, urine dip positive for blood but no RBCs on microscopy) → **Intravascular haemolysis** (e.g., transfusion reaction, G6PD; LDH very high, haptoglobin low, RBC fragments on film)
- **Viral myositis** (influenza B, Coxsackie; usually lower CK, preceding coryzal symptoms) → **Polymyositis** (subacute, symmetric proximal weakness, high CK but often with skin rash or anti-Jo-1 antibodies)
- **Concentrated urine** (no symptoms, no CK rise) vs **bilirubinuria** (yellow-brown, liver disease)
- **Splitter:** Urine dipstick positive for blood *without* red cells on microscopy → myoglobinuria (rhabdo) vs haemoglobinuria (haemolysis). If RBCs present → look for lower urinary tract cause.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:** urine dip (blood positive, no RBCs), ECG (hyperkalaemia), test for compartment syndrome (measure compartment pressure if clinical suspicion)
- **Bloods:** CK (key – >5× upper limit normal confirms; severe >5000 IU/L), U+E (K+, Cr, urea, Ca²⁺ – hypocalcaemia early), LFTs (AST/ALT from muscle), FBC, glucose, venous/arterial blood gas (metabolic acidosis, K+)
- **Imaging:** none first-line; consider MRI for muscle oedema if diagnosis unclear; US for abscess only if focal signs
- **Special tests:** urine myoglobin (unnecessary if CK elevated); consider drug screen (statins, cocaine, MDMA), TFTs (hypothyroidism can cause myopathy)
- **Diagnostic criteria:** CK >5× ULN with compatible history; severe rhabdomyolysis defined as CK >5000 IU/L
- **Never-miss test:** **ECG** – mandatory before any treatment to rule out hyperkalaemia
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - Secure IV access, start 0.9% saline 1 L over 1 hour (repeat if no signs of pulmonary oedema)  
  - Treat hyperkalaemia immediately if ECG changes: IV calcium gluconate 10% 10 mL over 2-3 min, then IV insulin 10 units + 50 mL 50% glucose over 15-30 min, plus salbutamol nebulised 5-10 mg  
  - If compartment syndrome suspected → call orthopaedics, measure compartment pressure, prepare for fasciotomy
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**  
  - Continue aggressive IV 0.9% saline at [check local policy – typical initial rate 1 L/1-2 h then titrate to urine output 200-300 mL/h]  
  - Monitor urine output hourly (insert urinary catheter if unable to void or severe oliguria)  
  - Check K+ and CK every 4-6 hours initially; reduce frequency as CK stabilises  
  - Avoid nephrotoxins (NSAIDs, ACEi/ARBs, IV contrast if possible)  
  - Consider sodium bicarbonate 1.26% or 8.4% [check local policy] if metabolic acidosis with pH <7.2 or CK >10,000 (weak evidence) – use with caution, may worsen hypocalcaemia  
  - If oliguric despite adequate volume resuscitation (urine <0.5 mL/kg/h after 2L saline) → senior review for renal replacement therapy
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - Identify and stop causative drug (e.g., statins, cocaine, antipsychotics)  
  - If recurrent episodes → investigate underlying myopathy (CPT2 deficiency, McArdle’s disease)  
  - Physiotherapy for muscle weakness
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - **IV fluids:** 0.9% saline (NOT Hartmann’s or Plasmalyte – these contain potassium) at rate aiming for urine output 200-300 mL/h  
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** LMWH (enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily – [check local policy] for dose if low eGFR)  
  - **Diet:** oral intake as tolerated; if severe AKI may need low-K+ diet  
  - **Observations:** hourly urine output, 4-hourly vital signs (including NEWS2), daily weight, daily U+E and CK until trending down
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents for hyperkalaemia:**
  - Calcium gluconate 10%: 10 mL IV over 2-3 min (if ECG changes)
  - Insulin (Actrapid) 10 units IV + glucose 50% 50 mL IV over 15-30 min
  - Salbutamol nebulised 5-10 mg
- **Fluid therapy:** 0.9% sodium chloride IV – rate [check local policy – typical initial 1 L/1 h; then adjust to urine output 200-300 mL/h]
- **Sodium bicarbonate (if used):** 1.26% or 8.4% IV [check local policy – dose depends on weight and base deficit]
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** LMWH dose adjust if eGFR <30 mL/min; bicarbonate dose may need reduction; avoid NSAIDs entirely in renal impairment
- **Most dangerous dose error:** ** Giving potassium-containing fluids (Hartmann’s, Plasmalyte, or KCl added to saline) – always use 0.9% saline exclusively. Also: over-aggressive fluids in oliguric patient causing pulmonary oedema – monitor urine output and check for crackles.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**  
  - Acute kidney injury (risk increases with CK >5000)  
  - Hyperkalaemia → cardiac arrest  
  - Hypocalcaemia (usually asymptomatic, check for Trousseau/Chvostek signs)  
  - Acute compartment syndrome – requires fasciotomy  
  - Disseminated intravascular coagulation (rare)
- **Chronic:**  
  - Chronic kidney disease after severe AKI  
  - Persistent muscle weakness or myopathy  
  - Recurrence if underlying metabolic myopathy untreated
sections.Prognosis: - Most patients recover fully with aggressive fluid therapy; AKI is usually reversible but may require temporary haemodialysis. Mortality ~5% (higher with severe hyperkalaemia, multiorgan failure, or myoglobinuric renal failure). Recovery of CK and renal function over days to weeks.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always check ECG and treat hyperkalaemia immediately – before waiting for blood results.**  
- **Start IV 0.9% saline at 1 L/h on suspicion – do not wait for CK result.**  
- **Measure compartment pressures in any patient with limb pain out of proportion to exam (use Stryker monitor if available).**
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not give fluids containing potassium (Hartmann’s, Plasmalyte, any fluid with KCl) – use only 0.9% saline.**  
- **Do not use loop diuretics to force urine output – they do not prevent AKI and can worsen hypovolaemia.**  
- **Do not delay fluid resuscitation while waiting for CK confirmation – severe hyperkalaemia and AKI can develop quickly.**
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** worsening muscle pain or weakness, inability to pass urine for >8 hours, dark urine reappears, new leg swelling, numbness, or tingling.  
- **Follow-up:** GP in 2-3 days for repeat U+E and CK; if statin-induced, discuss restarting (usually after 4-6 weeks with lower dose/alternative statin).
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- Call senior / renal team if:  
  - CK >5000 with AKI (Cr rising)  
  - Hyperkalaemia >6.5 mmol/L uncontrolled by initial measures  
  - Urine output <0.5 mL/kg/h despite 2 L saline (or <200 mL over 2 hours)  
  - Suspicion of compartment syndrome (call orthopaedics immediately)  
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or lactate >4 mmol/L  
  - GCS <15 or metabolic acidosis pH <7.2  
  - Requires ICU if: haemodynamic instability, severe hyperkalaemia not responsive, need for urgent dialysis, respiratory compromise.

rawMarkdown:
## Rhabdomyolysis
**One-liner:** Muscle cell death releasing intracellular contents, causing AKI, life-threatening hyperkalaemia, and compartment syndrome; often from crush, statins, or extreme exertion.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Hyperkalaemia** – ECG: tall peaked T waves, wide QRS, sine wave → cardiac arrest. Single finding: ECG changes despite normal K+ (rapid release, check urgently). Treat immediately without waiting for labs.
- **Acute compartment syndrome** – Tense, swollen muscle compartment; pain on passive stretch; paraesthesia or reduced pulse (late). Single finding: pain out of proportion to clinical appearance. Needs immediate surgical fasciotomy referral.

### Presentation
- Cardinal symptoms: severe muscle pain (often thighs, calves, lower back), weakness, and dark red-brown/tea-coloured urine
- Key exam findings: muscle tenderness and swelling; signs of hypovolaemia (tachycardia, dry mucous membranes, oliguria); check for compartment syndrome (tense swelling, pain on stretch, neurovascular compromise)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Rhabdomyolysis** (CK very high, urine dip positive for blood but no RBCs on microscopy) → **Intravascular haemolysis** (e.g., transfusion reaction, G6PD; LDH very high, haptoglobin low, RBC fragments on film)
- **Viral myositis** (influenza B, Coxsackie; usually lower CK, preceding coryzal symptoms) → **Polymyositis** (subacute, symmetric proximal weakness, high CK but often with skin rash or anti-Jo-1 antibodies)
- **Concentrated urine** (no symptoms, no CK rise) vs **bilirubinuria** (yellow-brown, liver disease)
- **Splitter:** Urine dipstick positive for blood *without* red cells on microscopy → myoglobinuria (rhabdo) vs haemoglobinuria (haemolysis). If RBCs present → look for lower urinary tract cause.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:** urine dip (blood positive, no RBCs), ECG (hyperkalaemia), test for compartment syndrome (measure compartment pressure if clinical suspicion)
- **Bloods:** CK (key – >5× upper limit normal confirms; severe >5000 IU/L), U+E (K+, Cr, urea, Ca²⁺ – hypocalcaemia early), LFTs (AST/ALT from muscle), FBC, glucose, venous/arterial blood gas (metabolic acidosis, K+)
- **Imaging:** none first-line; consider MRI for muscle oedema if diagnosis unclear; US for abscess only if focal signs
- **Special tests:** urine myoglobin (unnecessary if CK elevated); consider drug screen (statins, cocaine, MDMA), TFTs (hypothyroidism can cause myopathy)
- **Diagnostic criteria:** CK >5× ULN with compatible history; severe rhabdomyolysis defined as CK >5000 IU/L
- **Never-miss test:** **ECG** – mandatory before any treatment to rule out hyperkalaemia

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - Secure IV access, start 0.9% saline 1 L over 1 hour (repeat if no signs of pulmonary oedema)  
  - Treat hyperkalaemia immediately if ECG changes: IV calcium gluconate 10% 10 mL over 2-3 min, then IV insulin 10 units + 50 mL 50% glucose over 15-30 min, plus salbutamol nebulised 5-10 mg  
  - If compartment syndrome suspected → call orthopaedics, measure compartment pressure, prepare for fasciotomy
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**  
  - Continue aggressive IV 0.9% saline at [check local policy – typical initial rate 1 L/1-2 h then titrate to urine output 200-300 mL/h]  
  - Monitor urine output hourly (insert urinary catheter if unable to void or severe oliguria)  
  - Check K+ and CK every 4-6 hours initially; reduce frequency as CK stabilises  
  - Avoid nephrotoxins (NSAIDs, ACEi/ARBs, IV contrast if possible)  
  - Consider sodium bicarbonate 1.26% or 8.4% [check local policy] if metabolic acidosis with pH <7.2 or CK >10,000 (weak evidence) – use with caution, may worsen hypocalcaemia  
  - If oliguric despite adequate volume resuscitation (urine <0.5 mL/kg/h after 2L saline) → senior review for renal replacement therapy
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - Identify and stop causative drug (e.g., statins, cocaine, antipsychotics)  
  - If recurrent episodes → investigate underlying myopathy (CPT2 deficiency, McArdle’s disease)  
  - Physiotherapy for muscle weakness
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - **IV fluids:** 0.9% saline (NOT Hartmann’s or Plasmalyte – these contain potassium) at rate aiming for urine output 200-300 mL/h  
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** LMWH (enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily – [check local policy] for dose if low eGFR)  
  - **Diet:** oral intake as tolerated; if severe AKI may need low-K+ diet  
  - **Observations:** hourly urine output, 4-hourly vital signs (including NEWS2), daily weight, daily U+E and CK until trending down

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents for hyperkalaemia:**
  - Calcium gluconate 10%: 10 mL IV over 2-3 min (if ECG changes)
  - Insulin (Actrapid) 10 units IV + glucose 50% 50 mL IV over 15-30 min
  - Salbutamol nebulised 5-10 mg
- **Fluid therapy:** 0.9% sodium chloride IV – rate [check local policy – typical initial 1 L/1 h; then adjust to urine output 200-300 mL/h]
- **Sodium bicarbonate (if used):** 1.26% or 8.4% IV [check local policy – dose depends on weight and base deficit]
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** LMWH dose adjust if eGFR <30 mL/min; bicarbonate dose may need reduction; avoid NSAIDs entirely in renal impairment
- **Most dangerous dose error:** ** Giving potassium-containing fluids (Hartmann’s, Plasmalyte, or KCl added to saline) – always use 0.9% saline exclusively. Also: over-aggressive fluids in oliguric patient causing pulmonary oedema – monitor urine output and check for crackles.

### Complications
- **Acute:**  
  - Acute kidney injury (risk increases with CK >5000)  
  - Hyperkalaemia → cardiac arrest  
  - Hypocalcaemia (usually asymptomatic, check for Trousseau/Chvostek signs)  
  - Acute compartment syndrome – requires fasciotomy  
  - Disseminated intravascular coagulation (rare)
- **Chronic:**  
  - Chronic kidney disease after severe AKI  
  - Persistent muscle weakness or myopathy  
  - Recurrence if underlying metabolic myopathy untreated

### Prognosis
- Most patients recover fully with aggressive fluid therapy; AKI is usually reversible but may require temporary haemodialysis. Mortality ~5% (higher with severe hyperkalaemia, multiorgan failure, or myoglobinuric renal failure). Recovery of CK and renal function over days to weeks.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always check ECG and treat hyperkalaemia immediately – before waiting for blood results.**  
- **Start IV 0.9% saline at 1 L/h on suspicion – do not wait for CK result.**  
- **Measure compartment pressures in any patient with limb pain out of proportion to exam (use Stryker monitor if available).**

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not give fluids containing potassium (Hartmann’s, Plasmalyte, any fluid with KCl) – use only 0.9% saline.**  
- **Do not use loop diuretics to force urine output – they do not prevent AKI and can worsen hypovolaemia.**  
- **Do not delay fluid resuscitation while waiting for CK confirmation – severe hyperkalaemia and AKI can develop quickly.**

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** worsening muscle pain or weakness, inability to pass urine for >8 hours, dark urine reappears, new leg swelling, numbness, or tingling.  
- **Follow-up:** GP in 2-3 days for repeat U+E and CK; if statin-induced, discuss restarting (usually after 4-6 weeks with lower dose/alternative statin).

### Escalation Criteria
- Call senior / renal team if:  
  - CK >5000 with AKI (Cr rising)  
  - Hyperkalaemia >6.5 mmol/L uncontrolled by initial measures  
  - Urine output <0.5 mL/kg/h despite 2 L saline (or <200 mL over 2 hours)  
  - Suspicion of compartment syndrome (call orthopaedics immediately)  
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or lactate >4 mmol/L  
  - GCS <15 or metabolic acidosis pH <7.2  
  - Requires ICU if: haemodynamic instability, severe hyperkalaemia not responsive, need for urgent dialysis, respiratory compromise.
